 "title": "NL's 0.28 is a share of a stock that regulation has frozen — growth migrates to self-build hyperscale the entrant reaches through a different (foreclosed) door",
 "narrative": "geo.NL looks like the model's safest node: Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But both measure the same thing — the existing colo stock — and that stock is capped: Amsterdam's 350 MVA ceiling and grid connections unavailable until 2030 freeze the region holding ~70% of Dutch colo capacity, while NL hyperscale self-build grows at a forecast ~26% CAGR against colo's ~3.5%. The 0.28 therefore describes where the market was, not where the next five years of PDU purchase orders will originate. Worse, the demand that does grow in NL arrives via hyperscale self-build procurement — exactly the framework-locked channel already flagged as least contestable — so the entrant's effective NL opportunity decays on two axes at once: the reachable segment shrinks and the growing segment is behind a closed door.",

    "text": "Amsterdam | DC Byte

# Amsterdam

Supply: 1,849 MW

Total Supply is the sum of four supply categories: Live, Under Construction, Committed, and Early Stage.

Facilities: 90

## Overview

Amsterdam is one of the most established data centre markets in the world thanks to its geographic position as a gateway to Europe and its world-class internet exchanges, particularly AMS-IX. Major growth was seen between 2010 and 2018 and was then curtailed by a moratorium in 2019. Since the moratorium was lifted in 2020, development has picked up but the rate of activity has reduced with other markets offering strong alternatives.

As with other markets, power availability is a particular challenge for the Amsterdam market, with demand for electricity outstripping supply and leading to ‘traffic jams’ on the power grid. For data centres, power supply is affecting the market with many power distributions firms saying that they have no power available until 2030, thus limiting development.

Significantly, since the start of 2024, an amended decree has meant that no new hyperscale data centres (<10 hectares/70+ MW) are allowed to be constructed in the Netherlands, except for a few locations in the municipalities of Het Hogeland (e.g. Eemshaven) and Hollands Kroon (e.g. Agriport). Further regulation is planned for the Municipality of Amsterdam with a recent draft umbrella zoning plan suggesting that 350 MVA is the new maximum connection capacity within the municipality until 2030. Additionally, "

The Netherlands, widely acclaimed for its tulips, windmills, and cheese, is also an important player in a different realm: the data center market. Due to its strategic geographical position, its advanced digital infrastructure and its strong commitment to sustainability, the Dutch data center market, particularly in Amsterdam, is significant on the global stage.

Notwithstanding its growth, the Dutch data center market faces various challenges, such as high energy costs, competition for skilled personnel, limited land availability for expansion, power grid congestion and regulatory constraints related to environmental impact. Given rising global demand for data centers, these challenges strain the country’s ability to meet growth expectations essential to keeping the Netherlands competitive in the sector.

One of the current impediments to industry growth is the scarcity of suitable locations in the Netherlands, especially because the Dutch government has imposed new rules for hyperscale data centers, and the Municipality of Amsterdam has issued a preparatory decision restricting the development of new data center projects.

# Wethouder: Amsterdam moet op slot voor datacenters tot 2035

Netcongestie maakt nieuwbouw en uitbreidingen onmogelijk

Het is de categorie nieuws waarmee Amsterdam tot ver buiten de landsgrenzen de pers zal halen. In en rond de hoofdstad zal er tot 2035 geen ruimte op het elektriciteitsnet voor datacenters worden vrijgemaakt. Dat is het voorstel van wethouder Pels waarover de gemeenteraad volgende maand vergadert.

Amsterdam en datacenters, het was ooit een combinatie die nooit de pers haalde. Dat er veel datacenters in en rond de stad waren was bekend, net als dat daardoor AMS-ix kon uitgroeien tot wat het nu is. Maar de pers toonde weinig belangstelling en de bedrijven deden ook geen enkele moeite buiten de techtitels genoemd te worden.

Dat beeld veranderd toen de MRA aan de rem trok en in 2019 een moratorium uitvaardigde. Dat betekende ongeveer zoveel als “geen nieuwe datacenters erbij, tenzij” En tenzij verwees naar reeds ingediende bouwplannen, werk dat al was bezig was et cetera.

Voor Amsterdam was dat niet voldoende. In 2020 werden voor het stadsgebied de teugels aangetrokken. Zelfs wat er gebouwd mocht worden werd afgeremd. De netcongestie nam niet af, alles behalve dat. Daarom kunnen plannen voor nieuwe datacenters en uitbouw van bestaande locaties sinds 2023 rekenen op een afwijzing. # Data centers in the Netherlands: A shifting landscape?

Written by: Leen Marel Ernst Haverkamp Nicole Lange

##### Introduction

In the Netherlands the landscape for data center developments and operations is shifting. There’s increasing discussion regarding the development of new data centers in the country. This is because of the impact of the booming data center market in the Netherlands and the effect it’s had on the landscape and energy infrastructure. The Dutch government has announced and will impose new stricter rules for (hyperscale) data centers in the Netherlands. Although, it seems that the discussion regarding data centers in the Netherlands has lost some of its nuance, it’s clear the approach towards data centers has changed and that – to be successful – developments of (hyperscale) data centers should consider sustainability and planning aspects. To deal with these new challenges, a good understanding of these aspects is key in making the right investment and development decisions. This article will provide an overview of the current Dutch data center policies and legislation at national, regional and local levels.

##### National development of new data center policy/regulations

National Strategy on Planning (NOVI)

This NOVI is broadly based on the Spatial Strategy Data Centers 2030 (Ruimtelijke Strategie Data centers).1 The policy decision "

# Amsterdam Moves to Lift Data Center Moratorium

##### FOLLOWING STEPS OF HAARLEMMERMEER

The Municipality of Amsterdam has come out with its proposal to resume issuance of data center construction permit within its governing region after the halt order in 2019.

As predicted, this announcement is coming few weeks after the Municipality of Haarlemmermeer published its new data center policy, as an effort to end the moratorium on data center construction which was initiated alongside Amsterdam Municipality.

The proposed Amsterdam data center policy provides an opportunity to develop new data centers in the region over the next 10 years, estimating an annual average growth of 67 MVA with a total availability of 670 MVA by 2030. “We are positive about the growth path in both proposals,” said Stijn Grove, director of Dutch Data Center Association (DDA). “It is good that there is now clarity again, that will also benefit the investment climate.”

At an estimated average annual growth of 70 MVA, the municipality of Haarlemmermeer will allow new data centers to be developed over the next 10 years on four specific locations including Polanenpark, Schiphol-Rijk, Schiphol Trade Park and Corneliahoeve.

# Datacenter Guide 2026: complete overview of the Dutch data center sector

The Datacenter Guide 2026 has been published. In this latest edition, the Dutch Data Center Association (DDA) brings together more operators and facilities than ever before, accounting for about 95% of the total data center capacity in the Netherlands. In doing so, the guide underscores the industry’s scale, maturity and strategic position.

Of the approximately 50 data center parties operating in the Netherlands, the majority have European origins: 37 organizations are from Europe, complemented by eight from the United States and four from the Asia-Pacific region. This mix reflects the international dynamics of the sector and shows how the Netherlands functions as a highly connected digital hub within Europe.

Practical overview of Dutch data center sector

In doing so, the guide provides readers with a clear and up-to-date overview of the parties enabling the digital economy in the Netherlands. At the same time, the publication serves as a practical handbook and reference work for anyone involved in IT infrastructure and data center choices.

Stroomtekort, lange vergunningstrajecten en personeelsgebrek remmen de groei, terwijl de Europese vraag naar rekencapaciteit door AI toeneemt.

Eind 2025 bereikte de Nederlandse markt ruim 1,5 gigawatt aan IT-vermogen, een stijging van zeven procent ten opzichte van het jaar daarvoor. Tot 2031 verwacht de DDA een gemiddelde jaarlijkse groei van twaalf procent, naar 2,8 gigawatt. Die groei blijft desondanks achter bij het Europese gemiddelde.

### Brand in Almere onderstreept kritieke rol

De brand in een datacenter in Almere in mei van dit jaar maakte zichtbaar hoe afhankelijk Nederland is van deze infrastructuur. Betalingsverkeer, onderwijs, zorg en overheidsdiensten vielen deels uit. De DDA stelt dat Nederland zijn datacentercapaciteit niet alleen weerbaar moet houden, maar ook moet blijven uitbreiden om digitaal soeverein en economisch concurrerend te blijven.

Die urgentie wordt versterkt door het EU Tech Sovereignty Package dat de Europese Commissie op 3 juni presenteerde. Via de ‘Cloud and AI Development Act’ zet Brussel in op een verdrievoudiging van de Europese datacentercapaciteit binnen vijf tot zeven jaar. Tot 2031 verwacht de DDA een gemiddelde jaarlijkse groei van twaalf procent"}],"status":"contested","statusRationale":"The regulatory trigger is strongly corroborated: multiple independent sources confirm Amsterdam's 350 MVA ceiling, grid unavailability to 2030 (and municipal proposals to 2035), and hyperscale siting restrictions. The hyperscale ~26% CAGR figure is directly confirmed by Mordor. However, the load-bearing premise that colo is a 'frozen stock' growing at only ~3.5% is contradicted by strong sources: Research&Markets shows Dutch colo growing 16.2% historically and 13.0% forward, and the DDA (the very source the hypothesis cites as measuring frozen stock) reports the total market grew 7% in 2025 and forecasts 12% CAGR to 2031. This undermines the '3.5% frozen colo' vs 'growing self-build' dichotomy — colo demand is being redirected geographically (Middenmeer, Rotterdam) rather than frozen, and PDU demand can migrate with it. The contradiction hits a non-trivial link (the colo-stagnation premise) but the core insight — that Amsterdam concentration caps regional new-build and hyperscale self-build grows fastest through frameworked channels — survives.","evidence":[{"title":"Netherlands Data Center Colocation Market Size and Forecast Databook Q2 2026","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"ResearchAndMarkets","date":"2026","excerpt":"The colocation market in Netherlands has demonstrated consistent expansion during 2021-2025, recording a CAGR of 16.2%. This growth momentum is accelerate over the forecast period, with the market projected to register a 13.0% from 2026-2030.","url":"https://www.researchandmarkets.com/reports/6233880/netherlands-data-center-colocation-market-size"},{"title":"State of the Dutch Data Centers 2026 / DDA market report coverage","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"MSP Business (reporting DDA)","date":"2026-06-08","excerpt":"Eind 2025 bereikte de Nederlandse markt ruim 1,5 gigawatt aan IT-vermogen, een stijging van zeven procent ten opzichte van het jaar daarvoor. Tot 2031 verwacht de DDA een gemiddelde jaarlijkse groei van twaalf procent, naar 2,8 gigawatt.","url":"https://mspbusiness.com/markt-en-strategie/dda-stroomvoorziening-bepaalt-tempo-nederlandse-datacentergroei/"},{"title":"Amsterdam - DC Byte","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"DC Byte","date":"2025-01-19","excerpt":"350 MVA is the new maximum connection capacity within the municipality until 2030... no new hyperscale data centres are allowed to be constructed in the Netherlands, except for a few locations","url":"https://www.dcbyte.com/market/amsterdam/"}],"revisedLikelihood":0.32,"revisionRationale":"The regulatory cap and hyperscale ~26% CAGR premises are well-corroborated, but the hypothesis's colo growth assumption (~3.5%, 'frozen stock') is materially contradicted: independent sources show Dutch colo growing 13-16% and the DDA total market growing 7%/yr with 12% forward CAGR. Growth is being geographically redirected rather than frozen, meaning colo-channel PDU demand does not decay as sharply as claimed. This weakens the two-axis decay mechanism, lowering likelihood modestly while keeping it non-trivial given the confirmed Amsterdam concentration and self-build channel dynamics."}
